# MAIN ARTICLE Contents lists available at ScienceDirect # Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical j o u r nal homep age: www.elsevier.com/locate/snb # Montmorillonite-loaded ceria nanocomposites with superior peroxidase-like activity for rapid colorimetric detection of H2O2 Lifang Sun, Yanyuan Ding, Yanling Jiang, Qingyun Liu ∗ College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao266590, PR China # a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received9March2016Received in revised form13August2016Accepted17August2016Available online18August2016 Keywords: Montmorillonite CeO2Nanocomposites Peroxidase-like Colorimetric H2O2 # 1. Introduction It is well known that hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is not only the intermediate or final product in many processes such as food, medicine, enzyme, chemistry, biology, and environmental industry. Consequently, there are many analytical methods to be developed for monitoring of hydrogen peroxide, such as chemiluminescence [1], electrochemistry [2], fluorescence [3], bioelectrochemistry [4], liquid chromatography [5], and colorimetric assay [6]. Among these determined methodologies, the colorimetric assay has been drawn attention due to its absolute advantages in timesaver, low-cost, pretty simplicity and sensitivity as well as practical application. Furthermore, the detection of analytes using the colorimetric method can also be immediately interpreted by the naked eye to realize visual detection without any expensive or sophisticated instrumentation [7]. Recently, based on the colorimetric method described above, people have focused on artificial enzymes which are applied in biosensing and pharmaceutical processes, owing to their better design flexibility, high efficiency and substrate specificity, as well as excellent stability under mild reaction conditions, compared with that of natural enzymes. Because preparation of metal oxide in nanoscale becomes governable and the lattice matching between ∗ Corresponding author. E-mail address: qyliu@sdust.edu.cn (Q. Liu). # a b s t r a c t An efficient novel colorimetric sensor for H2O2based on peroxidase-like CeO2-montmorillonite (MMT) nanocomposites that were successfully prepared by a facile one-pot approach, was realized. In comparison with other nanomaterials as peroxidase mimics, the catalytic reaction by the well-dispersed CeO2-MMT nanocomposites was in accordance with typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics, which exhibited a higher affinity to3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2, bringing about a superior peroxidaselike activity (a distinct blue color change was observed rapidly within30s, by the naked eye.) than that of pure CeO2nanoparticles, MMT and their hybrid materials, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were used to establish a colorimetric biosensor for the detection of H2O2in a relative wide range of9 × 10 -6M to5 × 10 -4M with a lower detection limit of7.8 × 10 -6M. The sensor was successfully applied in H2O2detection in milk samples. © 2016Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. metal oxides is not difficult to achieve, more and more studies have reported that the nano-oxides can be used as artificial peroxidases to catalyze oxidation of colorless substrates and give different colored products in the presence of H2O2, which can be used to detect H2O2observed by the naked eye. For example, Fe3O4nanoparticles were firstly found to possess the peroxidase activity and were used to detection of H2O2 [8]. In the subsequent studies, various oxides including Co3O4nanoparticles [9], CuO nanoclusters [10], and V2O5nanowires [11] have also been proven to possess the effective activity of peroxidase mimics [12]. Among the various metal oxides, nanoceria has been widely applied in various fields, such as catalysis, electrochemistry, photochemistry, metal polishing agents, fuel cells, gas sensors, and luminescence, etc. [13-19]. In addition, it is known that ceria (CeO2) has a wide band gap (3.4eV) with high isoelectric point (IEP, 9.0) and excellent retention of biological activity for enzyme binding [20,21]. Furthermore, ceria has been considered as a promising material for the fabrication of mediator-less electrochemical biosensors because of its ability to act as a redox couple [22]. Just as the reported oxides, CeO2is capable of mimicking the activity of several natural redox enzymes, largely based on the Ce4+ ↔ Ce3+ redox switch in biomimetic catalytic applications, besides possessing the high biocompatibility [23-28]. However, pure CeO2nanoparticles with smaller size are easy to agglomerate, resulting in the decrease of their catalytic activity. In order to avoid of agglomeration of CeO2nanoparticles, it is necessary to find a support to disperse the nanoparticles. Montmorillonite (MMT), a layered material, has been widely applied in catalysis, separation, optical and electrical devices due to its versatile properties, such as large specific surface area, chemical and mechanical stability, layered structure and high cation exchange capacity [29]. Furthermore, the MMT layer consists of an octahedral sheet sandwiched between two opposing tetrahedral sheets (TOT), which is called a2:1phyllosilicate [30]. So far, a variety of nanoparticles have been prepared using MMT as a support, such as oxides (TiO2, ZnO and Al2O3) [31-34] and metals (Cu, Ag, Pd, Pt as well as Co) [35-38]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on preparation of CeO2nanoparticles supported with MMT. With these in mind, it is deduced that CeO2nanoparticles using MMT as a support maybe potentially possess the enhanced peroxidase activity in catalyzing the oxidation of substrates in the presence of H2O2. Herein, CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were prepared via a 'onestep' hydrothermal route and used as novel nanocatalysts to establish a novel H2O2sensor. In addition, in order to comparatively investigate, the pure CeO2NPs without MMT, and their hybrid material have been prepared under the vigorous stirring for3h, due to the large specific surface area and adsorbed properties of MMT. As a result, The CeO2-MMT nanocomposites demonstrated higher stability, dispersity and affinity to the peroxidase substrate3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were proved to possess the enhanced peroxidase activity and rapidly catalyze to oxidize TMB into a blue product in the present of H2O2, which can be observed by the naked eye. This method provides the sensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2. With the aid of this simple method, as low as7.8 𝛍 M H2O2could be detected with a wider linear range from9to500 𝛍 M. Thus, we develop a facile colorimetric method for H2O2detection based on the peroxidase activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites. # 2. Experimental section # 2.1. Materials Ce(NO3)3 · 6H2O, montmorillonite (MMT) and TMB were purchased from Solarbio (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen peroxide (30% wt%, H2O2), sodium acetate (NaAc) and acetic acid (HAc) were purchased from Gucheng Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China), Terephthalic acid (TA) was purchased from Chengdu cologne chemical reagent factory. The reagents, above all, were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. # 2.2. Characterization The crystal structures of the products were determined by means of the powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with graphite monochromatized Cu K ɑ radiation (D/Max2500PC, Rigaku). The morphology and shape of the nanoparticles were examined on the transmission electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of200kV (TEM JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). In addition, the Fluorometric data were recorded on a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (Varian, Inc, USA), and the UV-vis spectra were carried out by a MAPADA UV-3200PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The Micro-electrophoresis Apparatus-JS94H was used to measure zetapotential. # 2.3. Preparation of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites Firstly, 0.5g MMT was dissolved in30mL distilled water under the vigorous stirring for18h to form a homogeneous suspension. After that, 0.217g (ca. 0.5mM) Ce(NO3)3 · 6H2O solution as a precursor serving Ce3+ cation was dropwise added into the above solution under continuously stirring for another2h. Then, 2mL hydrogen Table1Different reaction systems.
Table 1 Different reaction systems.
NumberDifferent reaction systems
a0.2 mL CeO2-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
b0.2 mL CeO2-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1)
c0.2 mL MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
d0.2 mL CeO2 NPs (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
e0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
fNothing
peroxide (30%) was cautiously dropwise added into the obtained solution. After the solution being stirred for one more hour, the mixture was sealed in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at150 ◦ C for6h. Subsequently, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature, separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water for three times. Finally, the products were collected and dried under atmospheric condition at60 ◦ C for10h. The CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were obtained and used in the following experiments. For comparative investigation, we have prepared the CeO2NPs and CeO2-MMT hybrids, respectively. For example, the preparation of CeO2NPs was similar to that of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites in the absence of MMT. The CeO2-MMT hybrids were prepared by mixing MMT with CeO2NPs in the distilled water under vigorous stirring for3h. # 2.4. Catalytic oxidation of TMB by CeO2-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites was evaluated in measuring the catalytic capability of the peroxidase substrate TMB · 2HCl in the presence of H2O2. In a series of representative experiments, 0.2mL of TMB (1mM) in sodium acetate buffer solution (pH3.8) was dealt with different reaction systems, listed in Table1. Each of systems, the total volume2mL, was measured by monitoring the absorbance (at637nm) in time-scan mode and absorption spectra from300to800nm, respectively. # 2.5. Kinetics measurements The reaction kinetic measurements were recorded in time course mode on a UV-vis absorbance spectrophotometer. Catalytic experiments were carried out as follows: 0.3mg mL -1CeO2-MMT nanocomposites in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH3.8) treated with0.2mL of TMB (0.1-1mM) as a substrate and0.2mL of H2O2 (0.25M), or0.2mL of H2O2 (10-1000mM) as a substrate and TMB (1mM) with a total reaction volume of2mL at room temperature. The Michaelis-Menten constant ( Km ) was calculated by the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/ /GS = Km /Vmax (1/[S] + 1/Km ) , where /GS is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, Km is the Michaelis constant and [ S ] is the concentration of the substrate [39]. # 2.6. Detection of H2O2using CeO2-MMT nanocomposites The colorimetric detection of H2O2using CeO2-MMT nanocomposites as catalysts was investigated by measuring capability of the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB (0.2mL, 1mM) in the presence of H2O2 (0.2mL), together with1.4mL sodium acetate buffer solution (pH3.8) and0.2mL of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites solution (0.3mg · mL -1 ). Fig. 1. The XRD spectra of MMT (a), CeO2NPs (b) and CeO2-MMT nanocomposites (c), respectively. # 3. Results and discussion # 3.1. Structure characterization Fig. 1represents the XRD patterns of MMT (a), CeO2NPs (b), and CeO2-MMT nanocomposites (c), respectively. Fig. 1b shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared CeO2NPs without MMT as a support. The characteristic planes (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 0) were attributable to the face-centred cubic fluorite structure of CeO2NPs (JCPDS34-0394), which shows spiculate peaks related to the high crystallinity. From Fig. 1c, it can be seen that the characteristic but relatively weak peaks (marked with *) at2 /DC2 = 28.5 ◦ , 32.9 ◦ , 47.4 ◦ , 56.8 ◦ of CeO2from the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were in accordance with pure CeO2NPs (Fig. 1b), suggesting that CeO2NPs were deposited on the layers of MMT. The microstructure and morphology of the CeO2-MMT composites (Fig. 2A), the pure CeO2NPs (Fig. 3A) and hybrid materials (Fig. 3B) were imaged by TEM. From Fig. 2A, it can be seen that the CeO2nanoparticles with the mean size of ca. 10-25nm were well decorated on the layers of MMT. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2B reveals clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of1.63nm corresponding to (1 1 1) plane of CeO2. However, the pure CeO2NPs without MMT were easy to aggregate (Fig. 3A), due to the absence Fig. 2. Low magnification (A) and high magnification (B) TEM images of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites, respectively. Fig. 3. TEM images of CeO2NPs (A) and CeO2-MMT nanohybrids (B), respectively. Fig. 4. The photographs of the dispersity and stability of different materials dispersed in water initially (A) and after standing for3h (B), respectively. The different materials: CeO2NPs (a), MMT (b), CeO2-MMT hybrid materials (c) and CeO2-MMT nanocomposites (d), respectively. Table2The comparison of Zeta potential.

of a support. Moreover, the aggregated CeO2NPs had a relatively better physical dispersity on the layers of MMT by employing MMT as the support under continuous stirring for a relatively long time at room temperature, shown in Fig. 3B. The dispersed CeO2nanoparticles on layers of MMT are name as the CeO2-MMT nanohybrids. The dispersity and stability of different materials dispersed in aqueous solution were investigated. From Fig. 4, it can be observed that the successive sequence of the dispersity as well as stability in aqueous solution is CeO2-MMT composites (Fig. 4d) >CeO2-MMT hybrid material (Fig. 4c) >MMT (Fig. 4b) >CeO2NPs (Fig. 4a), which is further verified by the Zeta potential (absolute value), shown in Table2. As our known, the bigger the Zeta potential, the better the stability of the materials. Scheme1. Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of H2O2catalyzed by using CeO2-MMT nanocomposites. # 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites was evaluated by the catalytic oxidation of TMB, a representative peroxidase substrate, in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5a, when CeO2-MMT nanocomposites were added into H2O2-TMB system, a distinct blue change of the system (TMB + CeO2-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2) was observed rapidly within30s, by the naked eye (Scheme1). In contrast, the experimental system without CeO2MMT nanocomposites, shown in Fig. 5e, exhibits a negligible color change. These results indicate that CeO2-MMT nanocomposites possesses the superior peroxidase-like catalytic activity. In addition, the control experiments (Fig. 5a, c and d) showed that the catalytic activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites is higher than that of MMT or CeO2NPs alone. The reasons are as follows: One is probably due to the synergistic effects between MMT and CeO2NPs. The other is that the smaller CeO2nanoparticles with a stability in aqueous solution well dispersed onto the layers of MMT, shown as in Fig. 4. Moreover, it can be also seen that the system of TMB + CeO2-MMT nanocomposites did not demonstrate a color change, suggesting that the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites can not oxidize the substrate TMB without H2O2. Furthermore, from Fig. 6, it can be found that the substance with the highest peroxidase-like activity was CeO2-MMT nanocomposites, among four comparative materials in this study. After the aggregated CeO2NPs were well dispersed onto the layers of MMT (CeO2-MMT hybrids), the catalytic activity of CeO2-MMT hybrids were higher than that of pure CeO2NPs while still lower than that of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites obtained by a 'one-step' method. Fig. 5. (a) TMB + CeO2-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2, (b) TMB + CeO2-MMT nanocomposites, (c) TMB + MMT+ H2O2, (d) TMB + CeO2+ H2O2, (e) TMB + H2O2, and (f) TMB, respectively. Each of systems was measured by monitoring the absorbance data from300to800nm (A) and in time-scan mode at637nm (B), respectively. Fig. 6. The comparison of the peroxidase-like activity of different materials: CeO2NPs (a), MMT (b), CeO2-MMT hybrids (c) and CeO2-MMT nanocomposites (d), respectively. # 3.3. Experimental condition optimization Furthermore, the catalytic activity of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites depends on pH, temperature, the amount of catalysts and the concentration of H2O2, similar to that of the natural enzyme HRP and other nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics [40-43a]. The peroxidase-like activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites is investigated by varying the pH from1.7to8, the temperatures from23 ◦ C to70 ◦ C, the catalyst concentration from0to0.03mg mL -1and the H2O2concentration from2.5mM to250mM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7A, the catalytic activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites increased with increasing the pH from1.7to3.8, and then decreased. Thus, the optimal pH was approximately3.8, indicating that the oxidation of TMB occurs easily under acidic conditions [43b]. In addition, it may be ascribed to that ceria is unstable under strong acidic conditions to decompose the surface superabundant peroxide species into hydroxyl radicals [43c]. Besides, the preferable adsorption as well as chemical activity of MMT under strong acidic conditions are worth noting [43d]. From Fig. 7B, it can be seen that the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites remained the quite commendable catalytic activity from50 ◦ C to65 ◦ C, indicating that the nanocomposites possessed a higher temperature resistance. Fig. 7C shows that the reaction rate increased with the increase of the amount of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites. Similarly, the higher the concentration of H2O2, the higher the catalytic activity of nanocomposites. However, the rate growth became slow under high H2O2concentration, shown in Fig. 7D. In order to enhance the efficiency of the catalysts as well as avoid the self-decomposition of H2O2at higher concentrations, a H2O2concentration of25mM was adopted for the following assays [43e]. # 3.4. Kinetic studies of the peroxidase-like activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites To investigate the mechanism of the peroxidase activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters ( Km and Vmax) for the initial reaction that conformed to the typical Michaelis -Menten model were determined with a suit- Fig. 7. The peroxidase-like activity of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites depends on pH (A), temperature (B), catalyst concentration (C), and H2O2concentration (D), respectively. The maximum point in each curve (A-D) was set as100%. Fig. 8. Steady-state kinetic analysis utilizing the Michaelis -Menten model (A, B) and Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal model (C, D) for CeO2-MMT nanocomposites. The concentration of H2O2was0.25M while TMB concentration was altered (A, C). In turn, the concentration of TMB was1mM and H2O2concentration was varied (B, D). able concentration range of TMB or H2O2as substrates by changing the concentration of one substrate and keeping the other constant (Fig. 8A and8B). All of the parameters were also regressed to the Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal model (1/ v o vs1/[S]), which also gave the analogous values (Fig. 8C and D) [44]. As shown in Table3, the Km value of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites with H2O2as the substrate was3.399mM, slightly lower than that of horseradish peroxidase HRP reported in the previous publication [28], indicating that CeO2-MMT nanocomposites had a bit better affinity for H2O2than that of HRP. However, the Km value with TMB (1mM) as the substrate was0.0105mM, 41times lower than that of HRP (0.434mM) and much lower than other catalysts listed in Table3, suggesting that CeO2-MMT nanocomposites have a quite higher affinity to TMB. In addition, the pure MMT showed a higher affinity to the substrate TMB as well, due to the larger surface area of the layered MMT with a stronger adsorption ability to TMB. # 3.5. Mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites The previous studies have reported that the catalytic mechanism of nanocomposites is the generation of hydroxyl radicals ( · OH) from the catalytic decomposition of acidified H2O2 [46]. As mentioned above, it is believed that CeO2-MMT nanocomposites would facilitate the electron transfer between H2O2and TMB and then catalyze H2O2to decompose into hydroxyl radicals under acidic conditions, which oxidizes TMB into a blue product, as illustrated in Scheme1. To confirm this assumption, the · OH formation was evaluated by adding a fluorescent probe, the terephthalic acid (TA), into the reaction system of H2O2 + CeO2-MMT nanocomposite. The TA molecule easily reacted with · OH to form a strong fluorescent product, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which emitted unique fluorescence around435nm [47,48]. 0.35mM TA, 0.25M H2O2and the different amount of CeO2-MMT nanocompos-

Reaction conditions: TA (0.35mM), H2O2 (0.25M), and the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites with different concentration (0-700 𝛍 g mL -1 ) as the curves indicated (a-h) were incubated in acetate buffer (pH3.8, 100mM) at40 ◦ C for15min. ites have been incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH3.8) at40 ◦ C for15min. The incubated solutions were used to investigate fluorescence intensity. The results show that the fluorescent intensity of the experimental systems gradually enhanced with the increasing amount of CeO2-MMT nanocomposites (Fig. 9), suggesting that the more the nanocomposites, the more the produced · OH. # 3.6. Detection of H2O2using CeO2-MMT nanocomposites On account of the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites is H2O2concentration dependent, the system investigated above could be applied to detect H2O2with a simple colorimetric method. A typical dose-response curve at637nm depending on the concentration of H2O2from1 𝛍 M to800 𝛍 M under the optimal conditions was exhibited in Fig. 10A. From Fig. 10, it can be found that an obvious trend of absorbance gradually increased with increasing of H2O2concentration. The cor- Table4Comparison of the response of other H2O2sensors.

responding linear calibration plot is shown in Fig. 10B, revealing a good linear relationship between the absorbance of oxidized TMB and the concentration of H2O2in the range of9-500 𝛍 M with a detection limit of7.8 𝛍 M. Furthermore, the visualized variation could be observed by the naked eye, as shown in the inserted photographs of colored products corresponding to the different concentration of H2O2. In addition, we have summarized the detection of H2O2using CeO2and CeO2-based peroxidase mimetrics in this study and the previous reports, listed in Table4. From the reported data and our study in this paper, it can be determined that CeO2-MMT nanocomposites have a lower detection limit and a wider dynamic range of H2O2in comparison with other peroxidase mimics by the same simple colorimetric method [12,45,49]. Of course, much lower detection limit of H2O2based on other nanomaterials as peroxidase were reported, mainly because the different test method, an electrochemical method [50], was used. It is universally acknowledged that residual H2O2in the milk should be tightly controlled due to its harm to the skin and the human health, even though H2O2can be used as the stabilizer or preservative agent in commercial milk products. So, it is pretty important to detect H2O2in the milk industry during the course of storing the pasteurized milk for long duration [54]. Here, the practical application in detecting H2O2of real milk samples was executed under optimal conditions based on our proposed colorimetric method. Moreover, these analyses were performed for three Fig. 10. (A) A dose-response curve depending of the absorbance at637nm on the concentration of H2O2from1 𝛍 M to800 𝛍 M. (B) The corresponding linear calibration plot, in which, error bars represent the standard error originated from three repeated measurements. Table5shows the results of residual H2O2in milk samples. The recovery of H2O2in four milk samples is in the range from98.4% to102% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were as low as0.27%, indicating that our proposed colorimetric method demonstrates the feasibility and potential in real sample analysis. # 4. Conclusion In conclusion, CeO2-MMT nanocomposites firstly were prepared by a facile 'one-step' method and demonstrated to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which was much higher than that of pure CeO2NPs, pure MMT and CeO2MMT nanohybrids, respectively. CeO2-MMT nanocomposites displayed higher dispersity and stability in water. The catalytic behaviors of the nanocomposites were from the generation of the hydroxyl radicals. The activity of the artificial enzyme was dependent on the pH, temperature, H2O2and catalyst concentration. Additionally, the CeO2-MMT nanocomposites exhibited a higher affinity to TMB and H2O2. Furthermore, on the basis of the above results, a colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2is developed. The colorimetric assay demonstrates not only a higher sensitivity but also a wider responding range of H2O2, suggesting that the designed sensor of H2O2has a promising application in food detection, biochemical analysis and environmental monitoring. # Acknowledgements This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21271119). Actuators B230 (2016) 721-730. # Biographies Lifang Sun received her BE degree from Liaocheng University in2014. She has been studying for her master degree in the College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering at Shandong University of Science and Technology (SDUST) since2014. And her main research interests are focused on the synthesis of functional nanomaterials as sensors and their application in food chemistry. Yanyuan Ding is a master student under the supervision of Prof. Qingyun Liu in Shandong University of Science and Technology (SDUST). She received her BE degree from SDUST in2014. Her current research interests are the preparation of enzyme mimics and their sensing application. Yanling Jiang received her BE degree in Shandong University of Science and Technology (SDUST) in2013. Then she joined Prof. Qingyun Liu's group in SDUST and obtained her master degree in2015. Currently, she works in Jinan, Shandong province, China. Her research interests are focused on nanomaterial-based sensors. Prof. Qingyun Liu received his PhD degree from Shandong University, China. Following postdoctoral research in Ocean University of China, she successively joined Prof. Yansheng Yin group. Now she is a professor at Shandong University of Science & Technology, China. Her research interests include inorgainic-organic nanocomposites, colorimetric sensor, self-assembly of porphyrin and phthalocyanine.
